That's the easy answer to the question. But a slightly more interesting question is, what the hell are you guys doing here? Given that most of you presumably think you signed up for computer science. Or, as I said, if you have taste, mathematics and computer science. And the answer to that, well, there's a good answer and a bad answer to that question. The bad and slightly proximate answer to that question is because it's an accreditation requirement of your degree that you learn some law. The slightly better, and I'll come back to that in a second, the slightly better answer to that question is because actually it turns out that being a computer scientist does butt up against the law in a number of interesting ways, and it is helpful to your future avoiding getting into significant legal trouble during your career, to know something about those areas.

If you turn up, so well done, pay attention, read the notes, the assessment will be fine. Okay, so what are we actually going to do? We're going to compete with the great hallworks is what we're going to do. All that just adds to the entertainment. So we're going to cover in those six hours three bits of law. I'm going to do two of them and Eva-Alexandra is going to do another one. So two of them are areas of law which are, in some sense, special to computer science.

So the first one is copyright, and specifically copyright in software, because copyright in software is not the same as copyright in other things, exactly. The other one, which is specific to computer science, is the whole world of data protection, which is what Yves Alexandre is going to talk about, which is relatively new law, which has sprung up and is still struggling to deal with realities of the internet. And then the generic piece of law that we're going to deal with tomorrow, in the one hour I have with you tomorrow, is on the subject of contracts, which is not particularly IT specific, but totally pervades absolutely everything that you do in most areas of computer science, because it's the thing that regulates how all of commerce works.

And there are some interesting computer science wrinkles like how does a contract work with a web shop, for example. And so we will do that then. But today, for two hours, we're going to talk copyright. And the reason we're going to talk about copyright, obviously, is because you write software and software is covered by copyright. And what you can and cannot do in the process of writing software is primarily legally regulated by the law of copyright. And so if you don't care about this, then you're liable to get yourself into quite a lot of trouble during your professional career.

Actually, it's quite good news as well, because it turns out there's quite a lot of things that you might not have realized you are allowed to do that are, in fact, protected by copyright. So what is copyright? Copyright is a set of legal property rights, and we're going to come back in a second to what a property right is, because it's relevant here, in which the author of work, or maybe their employer, is granted by statute, by a piece of legislation that we'll talk about in a second.

And what it does is they allow the copyright owner to control certain acts with relation to that work, to that computer program, in the case we care about. And the original intent of copyright law, so copyright law basically goes back to the invention of the printing press. And the original intent of copyright law was to enable authors to make money from the printing press. It's very obvious how somebody with a printing press can make money from a printing press.

You print documents and you sell them. But all of a sudden, for the first time in human history, copying stuff is cheap. So that means that it's not very obvious how you make money out of the printing press as an author. Because... You can write a document and you can start printing and selling copies and anyone can take one of those copies and if they have a printing press, they can typeset it and start selling versions for you, which is pretty much exactly what happened.

In fact, the first... printed books were in fact already existing manuscripts that were reprinted. Contrary to popular belief, the first printed book in the Western world was not the Bible, because Bibles don't sell as well. And didn't even in the 16th century. So that's the big zoomed out picture, and at this point you haven't really understood anything. So now let's go into the detail a little bit of what I've just said. So the first interesting thing about copyright that will be quite different from the law of contracts tomorrow is copyright is surprisingly international.

So law is the archetypal national thing, right? You have a national government. The national government, in one way or another, specifies what the law is. And so maybe here we would talk about the law of the UK, or in some cases, England or Wales, because Scotland is a different legal system. But you might think, well, that's a slightly limited utility in a global institution like Imperial, where your next job might be somewhere else. But actually, when it comes to copyright law, there's quite a lot of international standardization.

And it's partly because modern copyright law is kind of pretty new by the standards of law. So there is an international convention which sets out most of it, which is the Convention for the Protection of Literary and Artistic Works, but everyone calls it the Bound Convention because it was signed in Bound in Switzerland, in 1886. and it does things like set out the minimum term of copyright and some of the coverage of copyright. You might notice that 1886 is an era in which there are a lot of computer programs, and we will come back to that in just a second.

Much more significantly, there is a... significant piece of European Union legislation, which is still British law under the provisions of the Withdrawal Act, which is the Directive on Legal Protection of Computer Programs of 2009. That's called the Software Directive. We're going to keep coming back and back and back again to the Software Directive, because for Europe, that basically sets what software copywriters.

Situation outside Europe can be a little bit different and in one important case we will actually see that it is different. Although even then the authorities are like roughly similar and certainly in most areas there isn't a huge gulf between Europe and the US which are the two biggest markets. So that really matters there. Okay, so that's where it comes from. Let's go right back to the beginning. So I said copyright is a legal property right.

So what on earth does that mean? So a property right is something that the law sets up. So it's something invented by the law. It's not a thing that you can find sitting out in nature. And it's not something that people made as a physical object. It's an idea. an idea created by law, and it has three distinctive characteristics.

And in fact, probably the one biggest distinctive characteristic of a property right is that a property right is enforceable against everyone. So what does that mean? Well, let's take a look at the most obvious case of a property right, which is not copyright. It's ownership of an actual thing. And ownership of land is one of the most obvious ones. So I, subject to my bank's mortgage, own a house. And what that means is that I have the legal right to exclude all of you from my house.

None of you agreed to that. We didn't have a contract where we agreed that you would stay out of my house. It flows from the property right. And the interesting thing about this is that that right is then amiable. That's the second characteristic of property law. That means it's transferable. So this gentleman here offers me a suitable amount of money. I might sell him my house. I transfer the property right.

And now magically, this gentleman here has acquired the right to exclude all of you and me from that property. So the key feature of property rights is that it's a right to exclude other people. The third important thing about property rights, which is critical to then actually using them in the economy, is that I can then waive them.

And an important point of waiving it is I can license them. So I could allow you to enter my house. And if I allow you to enter my house, I can't sue you for trespass for having entered my house, right? So by giving you permission to do something, I've changed the legal relationship between us. And all three of those things, so the applying to everyone, being allowable and being waivable, possibly via something called a license, so a license is the permission I give you to come onto my land, all of those things transfer over onto copyright.

And we'll see that in a second. Before I do that, I should flag the things we're not going to talk about. So people will talk a lot about the term IP, intellectual property. And copyright is an intellectual property right, right? So what lawyers mean when they say an intellectual property right is a property right which attaches to something immaterial, something that is a idea or a computer program which is, okay, a computer program is written down somewhere, a book is written down somewhere, but actually it isn't the physical artifact we're talking about protecting here, right?

It's that string of characters, however recorded. So it isn't the physical object, right? Owning a book is a different thing from owning copyright in that book. And so that is one form of intellectual property. People who aren't lawyers quite often use the term intellectual property in a really sloppy way, including to attempt to protect all sorts of things that aren't actually protected by the law. They claim they have intellectual property in something without actually grounding that in an actual property right.

And sometimes people will try and grab you into doing things because they claim intellectual property without really establishing what it is. Someone does waive intellectual property at you. You should always ask them which one. And often they'll shut up at that point. So the ones we're not going to talk about, we're not going to talk about trademark. So trademark is an intellectual copyright that protects a name or a mark, a logo. And the idea of trademark is it's to prevent passing off.

So it's to protect your goods or services from other people who would like to sell goods and services that they are claiming are your goods and services. This logo here indicates that Imperial College sent rather too much money to California for this laptop. And the trademark in that logo protects the Apple Corporation from other companies selling laptops and other goods that they pretend are from the Apple Corporation.

So that's trademark. We're not doing that one. This patent... So patent protects ideas. That's important because in a second we'll learn that copyright doesn't. So patent is about protecting ideas in inventions. Importantly, at least in Europe, and actually probably to a large extent in the US, although it is fuzzier in the US, this doesn't really hit us in straight-up computer science because you can't patent maths.

And computer science is just the embodiment of maths. So you might bump into patents if you are right at the hardware end and you can have mixtures of hardware and software that can somehow hit patent law. But as long as we're talking about software on its own, at least in Europe, no patents. There are also database... Yeah? Don't each country have its own patent office? Each country does have its own patent office, but Europe has a basically unified patent law. So how do, essentially, like, when you make patents, do you have to apply to essentially? You have to apply. So you can register. There are community patents in Europe. Otherwise, you have to register in every country. So yeah, patent law is tricky because you actually have to register. So trademark, you may or may not need to register. Copyright, you don't need to register. So it's easy. And yeah, so, but as I said, we're just not doing patents. This is not a patent law course. Did you say that You may or may not have to register a trademark. It's a bit complicated in trademark territory. You can also acquire trademarks by usage. So it's complicated.

And we're not doing trademarks, right? So there's also some of the database rights, which are an attempt to kind of adapt the concepts of copyright that don't really apply to databases to databases. And there are design rights. So if you're right at the hardware level, design rights are a special right that apply to the layout of computer chips. So if you are a polygon pusher, then you will bump into those ones. We're not going to do those ever, either. So what we are going to do is copyright. And I mentioned, right, so the starting point for this in modern copyright law, Brown Convention, 1886, and Ada Lovelace aside, not a lot of computer programming going on in the 19th century.

So what? Well, the answer... is, is this already on my next slide? Whoa, what's happened here? I'm in the wrong mode. Okay. No, it's not the next slide. Okay. So, well, it will be in a second. So, the answer is your computer program, as far as copyright law is concerned, is a book.

So there are different forms of work that are protected by copyright. So there are books, and plays are slightly different, and poems are slightly different again. And then there is music, which is protected by copyright. in both its written and recorded form. And all of those have different rights associated with them. And the way that copyright was shoehorned onto computer software is by court cases that ruled that essentially your computer program, initially the source listing, and then we'll worry about compilation and things in a minute, that is a book.

So the rules that apply to a computer programmer basically the rules that are applied to a book. Software directive then modifies that slightly, but it does feel slightly odd when we do that. And in fact, a while before the software directive, back in the 1980s, 90s, I apologize, there's a US case in which Judge Burden in a case between Lotus and Corlin.

So Lotus wrote the first spreadsheet. Lotus 1, 2, 3, and Lotus and Borland were slugging it out over copyright in spreadsheets. And the judge got quite frustrated in his judgment and wrote, the applying copyright law to computer programs is like assembling a jigsaw puzzle whose pieces do not quite fit. And that's quite true. Because the stuff that it is natural to do to a computer program where there isn't a plot There isn't a linear begin and end.

You can take the bits out and reuse them. In many respects, nothing about this is like a book. But the rules are books, right? So somehow we've got to make that work. And that causes an awful lot of confusion and difficulty and has done over time. But the starting points, the basic rights that you get under copyright are the ones for books. So the obvious one and the reason why it's called copyright is the right to make copies, whether permanent or temporary.

In particular, that includes the copies required to load, display, and run your program. So that means, so the original version of that is, you can't print my book without my permission. That's the basic original version of copyright. So you can't copy my program without permission. And the obvious version of that is, Particularly if you go back, say, 20 years or so to when computer programs were distributed on physical media, so on discs, CDs, or DVDs, or even floppy disks.

then you can't copy it onto the disk, and you can't copy it from the disk onto the new computer without permission. That's the basic idea. So you can't make copies to sell, and if you've bought a copy, you can only install it with permission, which basically might mean you can only install it on one machine, for example. And that's the original meaning. But interestingly, it also, so you can't run a piece of computer software without copying. Because it's sitting in whatever persistent storage your system has, and your processor is not in the persistent storage.

You have to copy all of those instructions onto the processor before they do anything. So actually, that original, you can't use your printing press on my book, also says you can't run the program without my permission. Now that's going to get more complicated, but that's the basic rule. The basic rule is the copyright holder has the right to control you copying the work and no displaying money. It has also the right to translate, adapt, arrange, or otherwise alter the program.

So the original meaning of that was slightly more obvious, right? So if I write a book in English, I also get to control whether or not you can translate that book into French or produce the play. or the film, or whatever. So that's translation and adaption. What that means for a computer program is, well, a compilation is basically automated translation.

A compiler takes a computer program written in one computer language and speeds you back a computer program written in another computer language, which in some respects is the same program as the original one. So it's kind of like the compiled program is the program translated into your choice of language you don't speak, unless anyone here is really good at Assembler. So that's actually kind of a pretty straightforward outcome of what we're doing, right?

Also applies to decompiling. We will come back to decompiling shortly. And then the right to distribute to the public, including rented copies of the program. That one is getting pretty close to dead letter now. So that was a deal when you were distributing physical copies. Because then the act of making the physical copies and the act of installing the physical copies is different from the act of, for example, transporting the digital copies into another market, putting them on sale and so on.

Because you can move around and sell the CD without making additional copies. So there are additional rights restricting that. That has pretty much gone now because... To all intents and purposes, all the software is distributed over the wire, and distributing over the wire necessarily involves copying, so it's already caught under number one. One might affect you in cases where you're shipping devices with embedded software. That would be the case where you would hit that one. So that's what copyright does for you.

So basically, that's actually most of the things you can do with a computer program. So owning the copyright in a computer program pretty much gets you there. That was problematic, not least because it means that in a whole bunch of circumstances, just doing stuff that you would naturally expect you can do, like run a piece of software that you have legally acquired, isn't possible without law permissions. And so that was legally inconvenient.

And legislators, when they're being good, attempts to write the law so that it actually works in the way that works for society and especially for the economy. And so in 2009, the European Union caught up with this and attempted to modernize software copyright basically in line with the way that the digital economy really works, and in some cases, the way that they would like it to work, which we'll come to in a second.

So the first one is under the software directive, the defaults changes. So under the software directive, if you have a copy of a piece of software which you have legally acquired, then the default position is whatever copies are required in order for you to actually run the software are completely okay. Now, that is subject to license, which means that the license terms can still take it away from you, and in particular, license terms that are restrictive of the purpose or who can run something do typically apply.

So if you have licenses that restrict something to non-commercial use, That is typically an actual restriction which applies. If you have licenses which only apply to you while you're a student, that also applies as well. But those have to then be specifically written in, right? The default position is you're allowed to run stuff, which is sensible and nice. The next one was more real in 2009 than it is now. The right to make a backup where necessary. So when is it necessary to make a backup?

Well, when software came on media that was quite degradable, the answer was if you wanted to keep using the software, you need to have backups. And so that was kind of what that was there for. What it means now is that merely the fact that your storage systems may be backed up probably doesn't infringe copyright. So many of the computer systems that you work on, you have network drives, those network drives are all automatically backed up. You can imagine that it would be spectacularly inconvenient if automated backup systems for massive network file systems had to worry about whether each file on there in its license permitted backups or not.

So it seems like the effect of that is that systems that are just backed up are going to be protected by that. And those backups are necessary in the sense that you couldn't use the software on that class of computer if you weren't allowed to backup. So that does what that one. The next ones are much, much, much more fun. So the right to observe, study, and test. So this one says, the person having a right to use a copy of a computer program shall be entitled, without authorization of the right holder...

to observe, study, or test the functioning of the program in order to determine the ideas and principles which underlie any element of the program. If he does so, while performing any of the acts of loading, displaying, running, transmitting, or storing the program, which he's entitled to do. So what that means is that if you've got legally a piece of software, you are entitled, no matter what it says, which is interesting because quite often the licenses will say you can't do this. you're entitled to, in the course of running it, observe anything you like in order to work out how it works.

So in particular, you can run it under a debugger. You can packet sniff all the packets that come in and out of the piece of software. You can do absolutely anything you like as long as what you're doing is just running the software and observing what it does in any way. And that is a really strong right. It applies basically in all circumstances. We're going to see a case in a bit where that had a really big impact. And then there's another one which is much, much, much more tight.

So there's also a right to decompile, but this one is really now. So the right to decompile. And decompiling is a painful and horrible thing to do anyway, because if you're... Playing your attention to Paul Kelly when he taught you compilers, what a compiler does is takes a higher level of abstraction and loads it to a lower level of abstraction. And a decompiler tries to go the other way. It doesn't work very well. And what you end up with is a complete mess. But it can be useful. It can help you to extract what the sequence of instructions in a program is in a readable way if you're prepared to work hard enough.

And you can do this, but it's only permitted in order to achieve interoperability and only if necessary. So the examples that kind of come up are if there is a server or a client and you want to write a corresponding work-alike client or a server, and, for example, they're trying to do the thing that's actually impossible, and they're trying to encrypt the connections by just leaving a key inside, well, you might need to extract that key in order to write a client that can interoperate, and that would be a circumstance where you might be able to decompile.

That one is a very, very narrow write. I would basically suggest that if you ever think that you're going to need to use that one, you need to get an IP lawyer first. because it's very, very narrow. Whereas the other one, observing, running debuggers, packet sniffers, all that, it's a really strong right. Go do it, have fun, it's fine. It's a very, very safe thing to do. The important thing about those last three, as I keep saying, is the first one is subject to license. The last three are always true.

And that's really important because especially the last two are ones that the copyright holder very, very often does not want you to do. There's a balance going on here. So on the one hand, copyright law wants to restrict to the person who wrote it the right to make copies. On the other hand, the legal system in general really dislikes anti-competitive behavior. And they don't want people using copyright to shut down whole areas and say, no, you can't build something which either interoperates with mine or competes with mine.
And so that's where that line's coming from. That's why those are in there. Okay, so a couple of other quick ones before we go forward. So the first one is how long is copyright? Yeah?
Let's say you write a piece of software and Is there a way to detect if people are decompiling?

Basically, no, but that's not a law question. That's a physics question. But you can do the thought experiment. I have your piece of software. I air gap the machine and decompile it. How are you going to know? Right, so no. You can attempt to bug your software so that it calls home when people start doing things, and their packet sniffer will go, oh, why is this thing suddenly opening network connections to the vendor's servers? And by the way, bugging your software is likely to get you into GDPR trouble, but I will leave that to Eve Alexander and his...

sanction. OK. So term of copyright. So original copyright terms were relatively short. So 14 years, 28 years when it was first done. Because, once again, of this trade-off. you want to incentivize authors to write works. On the other hand, if you shut down entire areas of work forever, then you shut out people building on other people. So if you want to look back at, say, West Side Story, which is Leonard Bernstein retelling Romeo and Juliet, should the estates of William Shakespeare, died in the 17th century, be able to stop Leonard Bernstein in the 20th century from writing stories based on the old ones.

That would be not in the interests of human culture as a whole, right? So the original idea is you get this for a while. And in some areas of intellectual property law, like patents, patents are by default 20 years, right? It's not actually all that long. So if you think about many of the things you use today, so any of the patents that might exist on USBA will be gone by now. Because USBA came out in the 90s. We're 30 years on. So, I mean, that hopefully was open license.

But you can see that that's the pace which things go. Copyright, on the other hand, has been subject to really extensive lobbying by rights holders to lengthen it. And we are now in both now the European Union and the US at life of the author plus 70 years. which is a very, very long time indeed, particularly if you are computer science. So this has become such a notorious act by copyright holders that, in fact, the US Copyright Term Extension Act of 1998 which is when they caught up with Europe at 5 plus 70 years, was widely termed the Mickey Mouse Protection Act.

Because it stopped Steamboat Willie, which is the first Mickey Mouse film from going out of copyright. Steamboat Willie did in fact go out of copyright last year. So if you want to do something based on Steamboat Willie, you now can. You have to be really careful, though, because the original Mickey Mouse character looks a bit different from the current one. And the current one is definitely still protected by trademark. But what that effectively means for software is copyright is forever.

So if you can find a piece of software written by Alan Turing, that piece of software came out of copyrights on, I did look this up, It was last year, June 7th, 2024. So almost exactly a year ago, right? So effectively that means everything that has ever been written in computer software is still in copyright and basically will be forever. Well, in the UK, the legislation that sets up copyright is the Copyright Designs and Paintings Act 1988, and the line on ownership is really easy, so we can just write it out. There is actually a term three to this, but it only applies if you're a government employee, so I left that off. So first ownership of copyright. The author of the work is the first owner of any copyright in it subject to the following provisions.

Two, where literally dramatic musical or artistic work or a film, so remember, software is a literary work, it's a book, is made by an employee in the course of his employment. His employer is the first owner of the copyright in the work subject to any agreements to the contrary. So frequently asked question at this point, of course, is always by students. So you wrote it, you own it. You wrote it, you own it, except unless you did it as an employee. Notice as an employee. So actually, if you're working as a contractor... This is one of the areas where you need to make sure that, or more importantly, probably the person who is contracting you, needs to make sure that you actually have an intellectual property agreement in place. Otherwise, you get into a complete mess where all the contractors who worked on a piece of software individually own the copyright in their contributions to the software.

So, and similar, yeah. Yes. Anyone want to answer this question? Correct. I told you it's weird, right? The software is a book. So software is a literary work for the purposes of copywriting. Doesn't have to make sense. It's just true. Right. Something else that kicks around and is a confusion, especially with patent, which came up earlier, is people will talk about whether something is copyrighted, or they don't even say that someone copyrighted something.

Not a thing. Copyright applies automatically. Copyright applies as soon as the work is fixed, which historically meant as soon as you set pen to paper. These days it means as soon as you type it into your editor. Because as soon as you type it into your editor, a copy of that exists in the computer's memory. There's not really a difference between the computer's RAM and anything more persistent. On some of your current devices, there may well really not be a difference between those two things. So as soon as you write it down, it's automatically covered.

Similarly, as soon as you compile something, copyright exists in the compiled work. And the copyright in the compiled work belongs to the author of the source code. It doesn't, in general, belong to the author of the compiler. It's an important point there. Okay. So that's the really basic stuff. Now we get into the bit where you have to start doing some thinking. It's all about the scope of copyright in software.

So which things are covered and which things aren't. What are you allowed to do? What are you not allowed to do? And now, this is where you bump into the limits of statute law. So statute law writes down various things you can and can't do, largely with reference to books. And when we come to working out what that means for a computer program, what we need to do is look to what the courts have said in these cases. In English law, that actually has a formal legal status that we'll discuss tomorrow when I do contracts.

But for current purposes, we can just accept that what we do is we look at the legal cases. So the first one is, okay, so how much copying is going to infringe copyright? So there are some really obvious ones, right? So there are some very, very minimal pieces of software where if you wrote a... If I sent you the task of writing, say, a recursive depth-first tree visitor in Python, and I told you all to do it, I would probably get a large number of essentially identical submissions.

Because there isn't actually very many ways of doing it. And this is where we bump immediately into a core idea of copyright in software. Rotation in accordance with this directive, so this is from the software directive rather than the Copyright Science and Patents Act, but there's an equivalent clause in the CPDA. Rotation in accordance with this directive shall apply to the expression in any form of a computer program, ideas and principles which underlie any element of the computer program, including those which underlies interfaces and not protected by copyright under this directive.

Right, so what that means is that somehow it must be your expression in the code and not merely the functionality. So if the piece of code is so small that there's more or less only one way to do it or only a limited number of ways of doing it, it just won't attract protection. Importantly, also, in the exercise I just sent you, none of you will have actually copied. So that's another reason why. Well, I assume that none of you would have copied. So that's another reason why.

But even if you did actually copy from each other, because there's no expression there, that wouldn't enable you to do it. But if I have a significant body of work, and I copy it, well, that's not the same case, right? Because another person wouldn't come up with the same thing. There is only one way of doing it. It is legitimately somebody's expression that they wrote down that piece of software. How much? Hello? Lots? No? Okay, so there's a case on this. The case is Cantor Fitzgerald and Tradition.

It's an English case. And, oh yeah, so if you see cases written down, right, so it'll be party one, V, party two. And so Cantor, Fitzgerald, and Tradition. This was about some broking software. So this was FinTech. I mean, they didn't call it FinTech because the case was in the year 2000. The stuff happened in the 1990s. It was bond trading software. So, one of the most celebrated instances of this is the original IBM PC BIOS. So the IBM PC was kind of funny. The IBM PC is the ancestor of all of the Intel and AMD and ARM machines that are running things like Windows. So the X86, not ARM, the Intel and AMD ones, the X86 instruction set. And IBM, this is like massive mainframe company, An essentially renegade little bit of IBM made the IBM PC.

And they did it by picking up basically off-the-shelf components and ramming them together into a piece of software, a piece of hardware. And unlike, say, Apple, who were also around at the same time, this is the late 70s, Their plan was, we want to sell the PC, but we want to have a whole ecosystem of other peripherals that are out there. So hard disks, floppy drives, printers, joysticks, everything else that goes out there. And so we're going to have essentially an open platform.

The one thing we don't want is other people making the computer. Our computers, everybody else in the ecosystem. That's their plan. Well, the answer is that the BIOS, the thing that enables the hardware to talk to, the processors to talk to the peripheral hardware, that thing is software. We can protect that with copyright. You also, this is really low-level interoperability, right?

You basically got to show the source code to this thing to the people writing the preference. So what they did was a tactic known as poisoning the well. What they did was they published their source code, but the published source code did not come with a license that enabled anyone to run it. You could buy licenses to run the object code. We bought the computer and it came with a license to run the object code. But you could buy this book. It was at that point a book.

It would have all the source code in it. And their plan was, well, A, people are going to build all these peripherals and have access to the source code. B, if somebody comes along and produces a rival machine, then we will just sue them and we'll say, well, in all probability, that BIOS code must have been copied from the book. How else would you get that? And so a company called Phoenix, I think. No, it was Compaq the first time around, and Phoenix was the second company to this. So Compaq, a company that eventually got swallowed up by Oracle.

Compaq worked out how you deal with this. What they did was they hired a bunch of programmers from other bits of the computer industry who were unlikely to have looked at IBM's work. And these guys formed the clean room. So they literally sit in a room and write the BIOS code. Then you have the dirty room. The dirty room contains computer scientists, programmers, who have access to the book. They use the book to write a specification, which they pass to the clean room.

The clean room implements that specification, which they hand back to the dirty room, and the dirty room works out what's not actually compatible, updates the specification, and hands the specification back to the clean room. So now you've got a mechanism whereby these guys, in all probability, are not actually copying the code. And remember, it's on IBM to prove that they are. But you've got an effective mechanism for getting to the point where you have an interoperating system.

That's called clean rooming. And it is standard industry practice. And that is, as a consequence of that court case not going anywhere, completely legal. We're now up to GPL version 3, which came out nearly 20 years ago. The GPL says is if you get a copy of this software, you can do whatever you like with it. You can run it for any purpose.

You can modify it however you like. Compile it, translate it, anything you like. But if you hand on copies that are either modified or unmodified, then the whole work, so the thing you got from me and all your additions, they all have to be under the terms of the same license and you have to provide source code to the people you hand it on to. So it has to stay in this open source world. Very different from BSD. And so that's the license that's on GCC, it's the license that's on the Linux kernel.

It was really successful. It's all over the place. it caused a little bit of a fracas with glibc. And so now, in order to, so basically you can't, in that world, redistribute binaries that were compiled against glibc.

because that binary is going to contain code that was compiled from glibc as well as code that was compiled from the original. So the compiler doesn't cause that problem, right? The compiler doesn't get ingested into the output code. But a standard library, like libc, absolutely does cause that problem, because it's just as much an input to the binary as the application code was. And so what are the options? So Richard Stallman's preferred option would be, that's fine because our world is free.

And people who wanted GCC to actually take over the world, which it did, said, no, we need to actually allow for commercial software to exist in this ecosystem as well. It's just whether the pointers are in user space or kernel space, but essentially the mechanism is exactly the same. And so the question is, if I'm dynamically linking, so once when I dynamically link, I compile the library, and I compile the object code, and I don't ever have a single binary.

Those are two separate things. And in principle, I can definitely use multiple application codes with the same library. That's the point of dynamic linking. I could also, if I'm careful enough about it, recompile a library and relink it a runtime against the same application code. So really, the library and the application code are independent. And then when I run them, I don't even really get a binary blob when I run them, right? Because the operating system's linker, dynamic linker, effectively loads the dynamic library into a separate memory location, then arranges the memory transactions so that I can call the things from the dynamic library.

So at no point is there really a combined work. And so... there isn't anything there to infringe copyright on. And now, in principle, you could have, because remember, clauses that restrict how you run a piece of software are usually legal. No restrictions on modifying the code. GPL restrictions only cut in at the point at which you want to redistribute.
